questions.scenario: A new Container Apps revision passes direct tests. The team wants limited real-user evidence before full release. Which traffic move fits the article?

options.text: Send a small percentage of traffic to the candidate and watch release signals.
Move 100 percent traffic immediately because direct tests passed.
Keep the revision at 0 percent forever to avoid risk.
Delete the old revision before testing the candidate.

questions.explanation: Small traffic percentages reduce blast radius while producing real evidence.

questions.scenario: An App Service staging slot works by direct URL, but production uses different sticky settings. What should happen before swap?

options.text: Review which settings stay with production and which move with the candidate.
Assume the direct URL test proves production behavior.
Copy every production setting into staging without a record.
Remove sticky settings so fewer values need review.

questions.explanation: Direct testing is useful, but slot-specific settings can change behavior during swap.

questions.scenario: Which are healthy reasons to pause a rollout?

options.text: The candidate has no named rollback target.
The smoke test does not cover the changed dependency.
Traffic moved but nobody is watching failure rate or latency.
The deployment used a managed Azure service.

questions.explanation: Missing rollback, weak tests, and no watch window are rollout risks. Managed service use is not a problem by itself.
